title: Detecting Cycles in an Undirected Graph: Fix the Low Link Values

statement.markdown: Given an undirected graph represented as an adjacency list, detect if it has a cycle using Tarjan's algorithm. The graph contains `n` nodes, each with a unique ID from `0` to `n-1`. The graph is connected and does not contain any self-loops. You can assume that the graph is represented as an adjacency list using a vector of vectors.
statement.inputFormat: The first line of input contains the number of nodes `n`. The following ``n-1`` lines describe the edges of the graph, where each line contains two space-separated integers representing the nodes connected by an edge.
statement.outputFormat: The output should be `1` if the graph has a cycle, and `0` otherwise.
statement.notes: You are allowed to use a recursive DFS function. You can assume that the input graph does not contain any zero-weight edges.

hints.text: Recall Tarjan's algorithm for detecting cycles in undirected graphs using DFS.

editorial.title: Detecting Cycles in an Undirected Graph: Optimal Solution
editorial.intuition: To detect cycles in an undirected graph, we can use a modified version of Tarjan's algorithm. We can use a depth-first search function to traverse the graph, and keep track of the low link values for each node.
editorial.optimalSolution: We can use a modified version of Tarjan's algorithm to detect cycles in the graph. We can keep track of the low link values for each node using a recursive DFS function. If we encounter a node that has a low link value less than its discovery time, we know that there is a cycle in the graph.
commonMistakes: Failing to handle the case where a node has a low link value less than its discovery time.
Using an incorrect algorithm to detect cycles.
